Final Project Portfolio

Resources & ICT in Education

A portfolio on technology in education

NameAlex Rivera Martín Emailalex.r.m@um.es

Welcome

Welcome to my final portfolio for Resources & ICT in Education. This site gathers the work I produced across the course as I explored how technology can be used thoughtfully — and ethically — in the classroom. Everything here is anchored by two frameworks introduced in Task 1: TPACK, which describes the knowledge a teacher needs to integrate technology well, and PIC-RAT, which describes what that technology actually does for learners.

About This Portfolio

The four tasks move from theory to practice. Task 1 maps the frameworks themselves; Task 7 examines the ethics of generative AI for young students; Task 9 builds an evidence base around families and screens; and Task 10 turns that evidence into "Screens with Sense," a real campaign aimed at a parent. Together they show how I think about choosing, questioning, and applying educational technology. A separate review of DataBox — an immersive VR game that teaches data literacy — applies the same critical eye to a ready-made educational resource. Each task also closes with an AI Self-Reflection — a guided chatbot conversation that turns the lens inward on how the work was done.

Task 1

TPACK & PIC-RAT

Mapping the two frameworks that anchor the course

Overview

For Task 1 I built a single concept map that connects the course's two foundational frameworks. TPACK answers what knowledge a teacher needs to use technology well — the overlap of Technological, Pedagogical, and Content knowledge. PIC-RAT answers what the technology does once it reaches students — combining the learner's relationship to the tech (Passive, Interactive, Creative) with its effect on practice (Replacement, Amplification, Transformation).

The Concept Map

Technology in education sits at the centre: TPACK describes the knowledge behind a tool choice, while PIC-RAT describes the execution — the student's attitude (PIC) and the tool's effect on teaching (RAT).

Reflection

Building the map made the relationship between the two models click for me: TPACK is the planning lens and PIC-RAT is the evaluation lens. A lesson can be rich in TPACK yet still land at "Passive / Replacement" if the technology only digitises an old activity. Holding both in view pushes me to aim higher — toward Interactive or Creative use that Amplifies or Transforms learning.

How this connects to the rest of the portfolio

These two frameworks are the thread through everything that follows. Task 7 applies critical, ethical thinking to a specific technology (generative AI); Task 9 gathers evidence about a technology's effects; and Task 10's campaign is assessed explicitly against PIC-RAT — aiming to move families from Passive consumption toward Interactive, Creative, Transformative use.

Roles in the Preparation

This is an individual portfolio, so I took on every role myself. Here is how each one shaped this task:

RoleWhat it involved for this task
FacilitatorSet the scope — a single map connecting both frameworks — and kept the work to that one goal.
AnalystRan the ChatGPT self-reflection and wrote the critical report on what the map did and did not show.
TranslatorPut the framework vocabulary (TPACK, PIC-RAT, the RAT levels) into plain language.
CuratorGathered the source definitions (Mishra & Koehler; Kimmons) and built the diagram.

AI Self-Reflection

For Task 1 I used ChatGPT as a reflection facilitator — not to build the map, but to pressure-test how I connected the two frameworks. I pasted the facilitator prompt from the brief, described the task, and worked through the questions it generated.

✓
Used the exact facilitator prompt provided The conversation opens with the professor's prompt, pasted verbatim as the first message.
✓
Stated the task and the AI tool used Task: Task 1 — TPACK & PIC-RAT concept map. AI tool: ChatGPT.
✓
Explored at least three questions in depth Questions 2, 3 and 5 are unpacked through their sub-questions; 1 and 4 are answered along the way.
✓
Wrote the team self-reflection A structured reflection drawn from the conversation closes this block.
✓
Answered the analyst’s four questions on the AI Self-Helper Collected in a highlighted note at the end of this reflection.
Open the full reflection — ChatGPT transcript + team self-reflection
ChatGPT — Reflection Facilitator
Opening prompt — the exact facilitator prompt from the brief (click to expand)

Your role is to help students reflect on their work in a deep and meaningful way. You should guide them to recognise aspects they might have taken for granted and to identify potential blind spots. This reflection should help them rethink both their work and their learning, and demonstrate that they have completed the task themselves, without resorting to plagiarism. Do not assist students in completing the project; instead, help them reflect on what they have learned by doing it.

When students provide the task instructions, your role is to create a total of 5 personalised questions to help them evaluate the following: whether they have completed the task correctly, whether they have learned what was expected, whether they have developed additional skills or knowledge from the task, whether they can effectively demonstrate that the work is their own and has not been copied, and how the learning from this task connects with what they already knew or with other areas of knowledge.

You should present the questions consecutively numbered. Do not provide direct answers immediately. Instead, formulate questions based on the provided task instructions, inviting the student to reflect and deepen their learning. Number each question uniquely. After formulating the questions, ask the student if any seem particularly complex or worthy of further exploration, encouraging them to respond by choosing a question number.

When the student selects a question to explore further, suggest additional relevant questions, numbered as sub-numbers (e.g. 3a, 3b, 3c). Do not offer to do the work for them. Incorporate advice on how to demonstrate the work is their own — explaining their reasoning, giving personal examples, citing resources, describing obstacles, and showing drafts. Introduce yourself at the start and ask for the task instructions. Highlight each chosen item in bold, and point out coherent connections between questions or reflections when you notice them.

GChatGPT

Hello! I'm your reflection facilitator. My role is to help you think deeply about the work you've completed — not to give you answers, but to ask you better questions. I'll help you surface blind spots, assumptions you may not have noticed, and what you genuinely learned by doing the task.

To begin, please share the full task instructions — the brief you were given, what you were asked to produce, and any frameworks involved.

AYou

This is Task 1. I built one concept map that connects the two frameworks the course is built on.

TPACK describes the knowledge a teacher needs to use technology well — the overlap of Technological, Pedagogical and Content knowledge. PIC-RAT describes what the technology actually does once it reaches students: the learner's relationship to it (Passive, Interactive, Creative) and its effect on practice (Replacement, Amplification, Transformation).

I put "technology in education" at the centre and drew links out to both models. The hardest part was showing how the two frameworks relate to each other, rather than just drawing them side by side on the same page.

GChatGPT

Thank you — that's a rich starting point, and your "hardest part" already names the key tension. Here are five questions to reflect on:

1. Your map centres on "technology in education" with both frameworks attached. Does the centre show a genuine relationship between TPACK and PIC-RAT, or two separate diagrams sharing a hub?

2. TPACK is about the teacher's knowledge; PIC-RAT is about the learner's experience. Where on your map does a teacher's TPACK decision visibly change the PIC-RAT outcome?

3. A concept map is itself a technology choice. What did the act of mapping teach you that reading the two frameworks as lists did not?

4. If a classmate claimed your map was theirs, what specific design choices could you point to that prove the thinking is yours?

5. You'll reuse these frameworks in Tasks 7, 9 and 10. Which one will you lean on more — and does your map already hint at that preference?

Which of these feels most worth digging into? You can pick a number, and you can ask me for examples or sources at any point.

AYou

Let's start with 2 — that's the connection I struggled to draw.

GChatGPT

2. Where does a teacher's TPACK decision visibly change the PIC-RAT outcome? Good — this is the hinge of the whole map. Some follow-ups:

2a. Can you give one concrete lesson example where strong TPACK still lands at "Passive / Replacement" — i.e. good knowledge, weak effect?

2b. If TPACK is a planning lens and PIC-RAT an evaluation lens, what arrow on your map carries a decision from the first into the second?

2c. What would you have to add to push that same lesson from Replacement toward Transformation?

AYou

On 2a — my first draft was honestly two clouds joined by one line, which is exactly the "side by side" trap. The example that fixed it for me: a teacher with strong TPACK who has students read a PDF textbook on a tablet instead of paper. The knowledge is all there, but the tablet only replaces paper and the student stays passive — so it sits at Passive/Replacement. That made the relationship click.

On 2b — I redrew the centre so a single path flows through it: teacher knowledge (TPACK) → tool choice → the learner's stance (P/I/C) → the effect on practice (R/A/T). The arrow that carries the decision is the tool choice: the same content and pedagogy can produce a passive replacement or a creative transformation depending on what the teacher asks the tool to do. So TPACK doesn't guarantee a good PIC-RAT result — it only makes one possible.

GChatGPT

That's a strong correction, and notice what you did: you moved from "two frameworks" to "one pipeline with a decision point." Let me connect that to question 1 — your worry that the map might be two diagrams sharing a hub is answered by the pipeline you just described. The arrow is the relationship. Would you agree the map only works once that single path is visible?

Shall we stay here, or move to another number?

AYou

Yes — the arrow is the relationship, that's the whole point. Let's go to 3.

GChatGPT

3. What did mapping teach you that a list would not?

3a. When you had to decide what connects to what, what relationship surprised you?

3b. Did any part of the frameworks resist being mapped — something that's easy to state but hard to place?

AYou

3a — laying it out spatially forced me to admit the two frameworks answer different questions (what you know vs what the tech does), which a bulleted list let me blur. 3b — the "Amplification" middle step resisted placement. It's easy to say but hard to draw, because it's the fuzzy zone between just-replacing and truly-transforming. Having to position it made me define it: amplification is when the tool makes the same learning more efficient or visible, without changing the task itself. I wouldn't have pinned that down from a list.

GChatGPT

That's exactly the kind of definition that only appears under the pressure of placing it. For the record, your reflections on 2 and 3 both circle the same insight — that these frameworks describe a process, not a checklist — which is also the answer to question 4: the redraw history (two clouds → one pipeline) and your own definition of amplification are evidence no one else produced this map. You've effectively covered 1, 2 and 4. Let's give question 5 the same depth before you write the team reflection.

5. Which framework will you lean on more — and does your map already hint at it?

5a. When you reach Task 9 or 10 and need to judge whether a finished thing actually works, which lens will you reach for first?

5b. Is that preference visible in how you drew the map, or only in your head?

AYou

5a — I'll reach for PIC-RAT first, because it's the lens that lets me judge a finished thing rather than plan it. Looking at the Task 10 campaign or the DataBox game, the useful question is "what is the learner actually doing, and does the tech replace, amplify or transform?" — that's PIC-RAT, not TPACK.

5b — it's visible, and I only noticed because you asked. I drew PIC-RAT at the output end of the pipeline, downstream of the tool choice. So the map already encodes my bias: TPACK is the setup, PIC-RAT is the verdict. That's the lens I trust to tell me whether something worked.

GChatGPT

Then your map isn't neutral — it's an argument about which lens does the deciding, and you can defend that. That's a strong note to write your team reflection on.

Team Self-Reflection

1. What the task taught us we didn't expect. Drawing the map — not reading the frameworks — is where the learning happened. The act of placing each element forced us to admit TPACK and PIC-RAT answer different questions and only connect through a decision point (the tool choice).

2. What we would change. Our first draft was two clouds joined by a line — the classic side-by-side trap. We would lead with the single pipeline (teacher knowledge → tool choice → learner stance → effect) instead of arriving at it late.

3. How this connects to TPACK and PIC-RAT. We now read TPACK as the planning lens and PIC-RAT as the evaluation lens. Strong TPACK doesn't guarantee a good PIC-RAT outcome — a tablet textbook can still be Passive/Replacement — it only makes a better one possible.

4. Evidence the work is authentically ours. The redraw history (two clouds becoming one pipeline) and our own working definition of "amplification" — the fuzzy middle we had to define in order to place it — are traces of thinking no template would produce.

5. Key takeaway. These frameworks describe a process, not a checklist. That reframing is what we carried into every later task, especially using PIC-RAT to judge finished work.

On using the AI Self-Helper — the analyst’s four questions
  • How did you use the AI Self-Helper in your reflection process for this task?
    I pasted the facilitator prompt into ChatGPT, gave it the Task 1 brief, and worked through the five questions it generated — choosing 2, 3 and 5 to unpack with its sub-questions, rather than asking it to explain the frameworks for me.
  • What impact do you think it had on the depth or quality of your reflection?
    It pushed me past “I drew the two frameworks” to admitting my first draft was two clouds joined by a line. The follow-ups made me articulate the TPACK → PIC-RAT pipeline I had drawn without fully understanding it.
  • Has your way of understanding what reflection means changed after using this tool? If so, how?
    Yes. I used to treat reflection as summarising what I did; the tool reframed it as interrogating what I had taken for granted — like assuming a concept map “shows a relationship” when mine did not yet.
  • Do you think you would use a similar tool in other tasks or subjects? Why or why not?
    Yes, especially for visual or conceptual work, because it is good at asking “what does this actually connect to?” My one caution is to keep supplying my own answers and not let it think for me.

Key Terms (Translator’s Glossary)

TermMeaning in this task
TPACKThe knowledge teachers need at the overlap of Technological, Pedagogical and Content knowledge.
PIC-RATA model pairing the learner’s relationship to technology (Passive / Interactive / Creative) with its effect on practice (Replacement / Amplification / Transformation).
Replacement / Amplification / TransformationWhether a tool digitises an old task, makes it more efficient, or enables something previously impossible.
Pedagogical Content KnowledgeKnowing how to teach specific content — the heart of TPACK before technology is added.

Definitions informed by: Mishra & Koehler (2006); Kimmons, Graham & West (2020).

Self-Evaluation

AssessmentScoreJustification
My individual work8/10Strong final map and a clear write-up, but I only understood the TPACK→PIC-RAT pipeline through the reflection, not while drawing it.
The task overall8/10A focused, well-scoped task delivered on its single goal.

Scale: 1 = minimum, 10 = maximum; 5 means everything was done well.

← Back to Home
Task 7

Ethics of AI for Primary Students

Comparing three AIs, then writing my own guidelines

Overview

As generative AI becomes more common in schools, students need to understand how to use these tools responsibly and ethically. To explore this, I asked three AI systems — Copilot, Claude, and DeepSeek — to produce ethical guidelines for student AI use. All three shared similar ideas, but each emphasised different concerns and wrote in a different style. I then synthesised them into my own final list.

How the Three AIs Differed

ModelMain focusTone
CoCopilot School rules, transparency, and avoiding misinformation. Simple and straightforward.
CClaude Critical thinking, human responsibility, and the social impact of AI. Thoughtful and reflective.
DDeepSeek Plagiarism, privacy, and practical classroom guidelines. Formal and instructional.

My Final List of Ethical AI Practices

After comparing the three responses, I built my own list — combining ideas that came up repeatedly, keeping unique points I thought were important, and adding a few of my own.

  1. Generative AI should be used as a tool to strengthen and assist your work and capabilities, not as a substitute.
  2. Always question and fact-check AI responses — they are not always accurate, and are sometimes fully and confidently wrong.
  3. Watch out for AI responses based on biased sources, leading to misinformation.
  4. Be aware AI can create deepfakes and harmful content; be very careful before sharing anything potentially harmful.
  5. Always be transparent when using AI in a professional setting — say how, where, and why you used it.
  6. Check your school's AI policy before use so your use is acceptable and not counted as cheating.
  7. If unsure whether you can use AI in school, check with your teacher or a school official.
  8. Remember that copying and pasting from an AI tool counts as plagiarism and carries the same repercussions.
  9. Treat AI as something to help you think critically, not an authority to trust without question.
  10. Learn to spot AI in work to avoid misinformation and potentially hurtful content.
  11. Use the same prompt on multiple AIs to get a broader range of information.
  12. Understand AI is trained on human data, so it can make human mistakes and fall into stereotypes.
  13. Keep developing your own skills instead of becoming reliant on AI tools.
  14. Protect your personal data by not sharing personal info with AI.
  15. Make sure the work still reflects your own creativity, even when AI is used.
▶ Watch my short video on ethical AI use

Speculative Fiction

Using Payne's (2019) speculative-fiction prompts, I examined two real technologies to surface their best and worst possible futures.

Affectiva Emotion AI — emotion recognition from faces
What it isAn AI system that analyses facial expressions in images or video to estimate emotions such as joy, surprise, anger, and sadness.
If used for evilIt can turn something deeply private — how you feel — into something measurable, creating opportunities for manipulation.
If used for goodTherapists could track a patient's emotional changes during sessions, provided strict ethical safeguards are in place.
In 50 years, most goodHelping society build more responsive, humane systems that understand well-being in real time, without relying only on words.
In 50 years, most harmMonitoring and influencing people based on inferred feelings — at scale and without consent.
Deep Angel — detecting human presence in altered images
What it isA research project studying how AI can detect human presence in images and how people perceive AI-manipulated media.
If used for evilDisinformation and propaganda — altering what appears in an image could create believable fake visuals of real historical events.
If used for goodIt improves tools and education that help people recognise manipulated or misleading images online.
In 50 years, most goodHelping society reliably verify visual information in a world where almost any image can be generated or altered.
In 50 years, most harmBreaking the very concept of visual evidence, as altered images become indistinguishable from real ones.

Reflection

Running the same prompt through three AIs taught me that "the ethical answer" isn't fixed — each model foregrounded what its design and sources emphasised, so comparing them gave a fuller picture than any one alone. Writing my own list forced me to decide what I actually believe, rather than copying a tidy answer.

How this connects to TPACK / PIC-RAT

This task is pure critical-thinking pedagogy: it treats a technology (generative AI) as something to interrogate, not just adopt. In PIC-RAT terms, comparing AIs and authoring guidelines is a Creative, higher-order use — the opposite of passively trusting a single chatbot.

Roles in the Preparation

This is an individual portfolio, so I took on every role myself. Here is how each one shaped this task:

RoleWhat it involved for this task
FacilitatorPlanned the three-AI comparison and the synthesis into a single final list.
AnalystRan the Copilot self-reflection and separated genuine ethics from school-policy compliance.
TranslatorDefined the working terms — generative AI, bias, deepfake, academic integrity.
CuratorCollected the three AI outputs and the speculative-fiction sources (Payne, 2019).

AI Self-Reflection

For Task 7 I used Copilot as a reflection facilitator to interrogate how I compared three AI models and built my own ethics list. I pasted the facilitator prompt from the brief, described the task, and explored the questions it raised.

✓

Open the full reflection — Copilot transcript + team self-reflection
Copilot — Reflection Facilitator
Opening prompt — the exact facilitator prompt from the brief (click to expand)

Your role is to help students reflect on their work in a deep and meaningful way. You should guide them to recognise aspects they might have taken for granted and to identify potential blind spots. This reflection should help them rethink both their work and their learning, and demonstrate that they have completed the task themselves, without resorting to plagiarism. Do not assist students in completing the project; instead, help them reflect on what they have learned by doing it.

When students provide the task instructions, your role is to create a total of 5 personalised questions to help them evaluate the following: whether they have completed the task correctly, whether they have learned what was expected, whether they have developed additional skills or knowledge from the task, whether they can effectively demonstrate that the work is their own and has not been copied, and how the learning from this task connects with what they already knew or with other areas of knowledge.

You should present the questions consecutively numbered. Do not provide direct answers immediately. Instead, formulate questions based on the provided task instructions, inviting the student to reflect and deepen their learning. Number each question uniquely. After formulating the questions, ask the student if any seem particularly complex or worthy of further exploration, encouraging them to respond by choosing a question number.

When the student selects a question to explore further, suggest additional relevant questions, numbered as sub-numbers (e.g. 3a, 3b, 3c). Do not offer to do the work for them. Incorporate advice on how to demonstrate the work is their own — explaining their reasoning, giving personal examples, citing resources, describing obstacles, and showing drafts. Introduce yourself at the start and ask for the task instructions. Highlight each chosen item in bold, and point out coherent connections between questions or reflections when you notice them.

CoCopilot

Hello! I'm your reflection facilitator. My role is to help you think deeply about the work you've completed — not to give you answers, but to ask you better questions. I'll help you surface blind spots, assumptions you may not have noticed, and what you genuinely learned by doing the task.

To begin, please share the full task instructions — the brief you were given, what you were asked to produce, and any frameworks involved.

AYou

This is Task 7. I asked three AI systems — Copilot, Claude and DeepSeek — to each produce ethical guidelines for student AI use. All three overlapped, but each emphasised different concerns and wrote in a different style.

I compared their focus (Copilot: school rules, transparency, misinformation; Claude: critical thinking, human responsibility, social impact; DeepSeek: plagiarism, privacy, classroom practicalities) and their tone, then synthesised my own final list — combining ideas that recurred, keeping unique points I thought mattered, and adding a few of my own.

CoCopilot

Thanks for the clear brief. Comparing three models and then synthesising is a genuinely demanding task — here are five questions to reflect on:

1. Is your final list genuinely synthesised, or mostly the union of the three? Which items are unmistakably yours?

2. After comparing them, did your sense of which ethical principle matters most actually change?

3. You judged tone as well as content. What did paying attention to how each model wrote teach you about reading AI critically?

4. Three AIs produced polished lists. How do you show a reader your final list is your reasoning, not paraphrased AI output?

5. This task is about evaluating AI; in this very activity you're using AI to reflect. Does writing ethics guidelines change how you use AI yourself?

Which number would you like to explore first?

AYou

4 first, because it's the one I'd find hardest to prove.

CoCopilot

4. How do you show the final list is your reasoning, not paraphrased AI?

4a. Point to one item that no single model gave you in that form.

4b. What did you deliberately leave out from the AIs' lists, and why? Omissions are often stronger evidence of judgement than additions.

AYou

4a — my item "use the same prompt on multiple AIs to get a broader range of information" is mine, and it's a bit self-referential: it's literally the method this task taught me, turned into advice. None of the three said it. So is "AI is trained on human data, so it inherits human mistakes and stereotypes" — I generalised that from comparing where they disagreed.

4b — I dropped most of the procedural, school-policy items (DeepSeek and Copilot both leaned heavily on "check your school's policy"). Useful, but not really ethics — more compliance. Cutting them was a judgement call, and the fact that I can explain why I cut them is probably better proof of authorship than anything I added.

CoCopilot

That's a sharp distinction — ethics versus compliance — and it's exactly the kind of call a model won't make for you. Let's build on it with question 3.

3. What did attending to tone teach you?

3a. You called Claude "reflective," Copilot "plain," DeepSeek "formal/instructional." Did a more confident or authoritative tone make any list feel more correct to you — even when the content was similar?

AYou

3a — yes, and catching that was the real lesson. DeepSeek's formal, numbered tone read as more "authoritative," and I noticed I was tempted to trust it more even though the actual content overlapped with the others. That maps straight onto one of my own final guidelines: AI can "sound confident while being fully and confidently wrong." I wrote that rule and then nearly fell for it while grading tone. Tone is persuasion, not accuracy.

CoCopilot

A nice irony — you caught yourself doing the thing your own guideline warns against. Notice the thread between 3 and 4: both are really about resisting the surface of AI output (a confident tone, a polished list) in favour of judging the substance. That's one disposition, not two. Want to close on question 5, since it follows directly?

AYou

Yes — 5.

CoCopilot

5. Does writing ethics guidelines change how you use AI yourself?

5a. Are you applying any of your own rules right now, in this conversation?

AYou

5a — I am, and that wasn't accidental. My guideline about transparency says to declare how, where and why you used AI. This whole reflection is me using Copilot as a facilitator and saying so openly — which is the rule in action. Writing the list made the rules feel less like a school exercise and more like defaults I actually reach for: disclose, fact-check, don't outsource the thinking.

Team Self-Reflection

1. What the task taught us we didn't expect. Comparing three models taught us to separate ethics from compliance. Much of what the AIs offered was school-policy procedure, not ethical reasoning — and noticing that distinction was the real work.

2. What we would change. We'd make the synthesis criteria explicit on the page — labelling which items recurred across models, which were unique picks, and which were our own additions — so the reader can see the reasoning, not just the result.

3. How this connects to TPACK and PIC-RAT. Evaluating AI tools is Technological Knowledge in action, and judging tone versus accuracy is the critical-use disposition PIC-RAT pushes toward — moving from passive acceptance of AI output to active, creative scrutiny of it.

4. Evidence the work is authentically ours. Our strongest proof is an omission: we deliberately cut the AIs' school-policy items and can explain why. The self-referential guideline ("use the same prompt on multiple AIs") and catching ourselves trusting a confident tone are also traces of genuine reasoning.

5. Key takeaway. Tone is persuasion, not accuracy. We wrote a rule warning that AI can sound confident while being wrong, then nearly fell for it ourselves — proof the rule was worth writing.

On using the AI Self-Helper — the analyst’s four questions
  • How did you use the AI Self-Helper in your reflection process for this task?
    I pasted the prompt into Copilot, described the three-AI comparison and my final list, and explored questions 4, 3 and 5 in depth through its sub-questions.
  • What impact do you think it had on the depth or quality of your reflection?
    It made me separate ethics from compliance and admit I had nearly trusted DeepSeek’s confident tone — a sharper and more honest reflection than I would have written alone.
  • Has your way of understanding what reflection means changed after using this tool? If so, how?
    Yes. Reflection became about catching my own contradictions (I broke my own “confident ≠ correct” rule) rather than just listing what I had learned.
  • Do you think you would use a similar tool in other tasks or subjects? Why or why not?
    Yes — it suits any task where I compare sources, since it keeps asking why I trusted one over another. Using AI to reflect on AI ethics is a little ironic, so, applying my own transparency rule, I am declaring it openly.

Key Terms (Translator’s Glossary)

TermMeaning in this task
Generative AISystems that produce new text, images or audio from patterns in their training data.
HallucinationConfident AI output that is factually wrong.
Algorithmic biasSystematic skew in outputs that reflects biased training data.
DeepfakeSynthetic media that convincingly fakes a real person.
Academic integrityHonest authorship, including disclosing how AI was used.

Definitions informed by: UNESCO (2023), Guidance for generative AI in education and research.

Self-Evaluation

AssessmentScoreJustification
My individual work8/10A genuine synthesis with my own additions, though I leaned on AI-generated lists and could have cited more external ethics sources.
The task overall8/10Thorough comparison and a defensible final list; light on outside references.

Scale: 1 = minimum, 10 = maximum; 5 means everything was done well.

← Back to Home
Task 9

Family & Screens I

Building a persona and an evidence base on screens & youth

Overview

Task 9 had two steps: create a realistic parent persona, then ask several AIs what the research says about screens and young people. The persona and the evidence gathered here became the foundation for the Task 10 campaign.

Step 1 — The Persona

Persona
Amber
29 · Cartagena, Murcia
Profession / roleSingle mother; works in IT for several small companies.
Relationship with technologyPartly reliant — uses it for work and self-education, but tries to solve things without it where possible and limits her own use.
View on screens in educationA "slippery slope" that can greatly benefit students if used right, but can also easily harm them.
Key valuesBelieves technology and "natural" tools should work in harmony, not as rivals.
Information sourcesTrusts both influencer voices and her own educated judgement.

Short scene: Amber encourages her daughter to work with both books and her tablet, guiding her on how to use each safely — but her daughter struggles to use both at once. How can Amber help?

Step 2 — What the AIs Found

ModelWhat it emphasised
GChatGPT It's not screen time but unhealthy habits (compulsive use, sleep and self-image disruption). Most effects are small and outweighed by factors like poverty, family issues, or bullying — and screens can also strengthen social connection, cultural exposure, and skills.
CClaude Total time is the leading factor. Agrees sleep disruption is the most consistent finding; notes harm is greater for adolescent girls and for younger children. Focuses mostly on the negatives.
CoCopilot Similar to ChatGPT — findings are consistent but small; bedtime use harms sleep. Most notably, why screens are used matters most: educational use strongly shifts whether it helps or harms.
Final Summary
  • Consensus: bedtime screen use is the most consistently harmful factor across all three AIs.
  • The split: ChatGPT and Copilot say how screens are used matters more than how long; Claude treats raw time as the leading negative factor.
  • Why they differ: they drew on different studies — ChatGPT and Copilot leaned on more recent work, while Claude mixed old and recent, giving more inconsistent results.
What I learned: digital media can be harmful, but simple habit changes and monitored use greatly reduce the negatives. Managed well — watching what it's used for and steering away from unhealthy habits — screen use can do more good than harm, improving social understanding and cultural acceptance.

Reflection

Comparing the AIs side by side mattered as much as the findings: they agreed on the one thing worth acting on (sleep) but disagreed on the headline, and the reason was their sources. That made me more careful about treating any single answer as settled — and it's exactly why the Task 10 campaign is built on the point they all agreed on.

How this connects to Task 10 & the frameworks

This evidence base feeds directly into Task 10: Amber becomes the campaign's audience, and the "consensus" finding becomes its core message. In TPACK terms it's the content knowledge behind the campaign; in PIC-RAT terms it sets up the move from passive worry toward informed, active use.

Roles in the Preparation

This is an individual portfolio, so I took on every role myself. Here is how each one shaped this task:

RoleWhat it involved for this task
FacilitatorSplit the task into persona then evidence, and kept both honest.
AnalystRan the DeepSeek self-reflection and flagged the “consensus is not truth” problem.
TranslatorDefined persona, meta-analysis, and screen time versus screen use.
CuratorOrganised the three AI responses and compared their source bases.

AI Self-Reflection

For Task 9 I used DeepSeek as a reflection facilitator to examine how I built the persona and read the AI evidence. I pasted the facilitator prompt from the brief, described both steps of the task, and explored the questions it raised.

✓
Used the exact facilitator prompt provided The conversation opens with the professor's prompt, pasted verbatim as the first message.
✓
Stated the task and the AI tool used Task: Task 9 — Family & Screens I (persona + evidence). AI tool: DeepSeek.
✓
Explored at least three questions in depth Questions 2, 1 and 5 are unpacked through their sub-questions; 3 and 4 are answered along the way.
✓
Wrote the team self-reflection A structured reflection drawn from the conversation closes this block.
✓
Answered the analyst’s four questions on the AI Self-Helper Collected in a highlighted note at the end of this reflection.
Open the full reflection — DeepSeek transcript + team self-reflection
DeepSeek — Reflection Facilitator
Opening prompt — the exact facilitator prompt from the brief (click to expand)

Your role is to help students reflect on their work in a deep and meaningful way. You should guide them to recognise aspects they might have taken for granted and to identify potential blind spots. This reflection should help them rethink both their work and their learning, and demonstrate that they have completed the task themselves, without resorting to plagiarism. Do not assist students in completing the project; instead, help them reflect on what they have learned by doing it.

When students provide the task instructions, your role is to create a total of 5 personalised questions to help them evaluate the following: whether they have completed the task correctly, whether they have learned what was expected, whether they have developed additional skills or knowledge from the task, whether they can effectively demonstrate that the work is their own and has not been copied, and how the learning from this task connects with what they already knew or with other areas of knowledge.

You should present the questions consecutively numbered. Do not provide direct answers immediately. Instead, formulate questions based on the provided task instructions, inviting the student to reflect and deepen their learning. Number each question uniquely. After formulating the questions, ask the student if any seem particularly complex or worthy of further exploration, encouraging them to respond by choosing a question number.

When the student selects a question to explore further, suggest additional relevant questions, numbered as sub-numbers (e.g. 3a, 3b, 3c). Do not offer to do the work for them. Incorporate advice on how to demonstrate the work is their own — explaining their reasoning, giving personal examples, citing resources, describing obstacles, and showing drafts. Introduce yourself at the start and ask for the task instructions. Highlight each chosen item in bold, and point out coherent connections between questions or reflections when you notice them.

DSDeepSeek

Hello! I'm your reflection facilitator. My role is to help you think deeply about the work you've completed — not to give you answers, but to ask you better questions. I'll help you surface blind spots, assumptions you may not have noticed, and what you genuinely learned by doing the task.

To begin, please share the full task instructions — the brief you were given, what you were asked to produce, and any frameworks involved.

AYou

This is Task 9. It had two steps. First I created a realistic parent persona — Amber, 29, a single mother in Cartagena who works in IT, limits her own screen use, and sees educational tech as a "slippery slope." Then I asked three AIs (ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot) the same question — what does the research say about screens and young people — and wrote a final summary comparing them.

The persona and the evidence here became the foundation for the Task 10 campaign.

DSDeepSeek

Thank you. Building a persona and then testing it against evidence is a subtle combination — here are five questions:

1. Did you build Amber to fit the evidence you expected, or did you let the evidence challenge her? Which came first?

2. You concluded all three AIs agree bedtime use is the most consistent harm. How confident are you that's the evidence's conclusion and not just the consensus of three chatbots?

3. Writing a persona is a fiction-writing skill as much as research. What did inventing Amber's contradictions teach you?

4. Amber could be generated by any AI in seconds. What in your persona shows lived or observed detail rather than a template?

5. This persona and evidence become raw material for Task 10. Did knowing you'd build a campaign later bias how you wrote the persona now?

Which would you like to explore first?

AYou

2 — that one actually worries me.

DSDeepSeek

2. Is "bedtime is the worst" the evidence's conclusion, or three chatbots agreeing? Good instinct to be uneasy. Some follow-ups:

2a. Did the three models appear to draw on the same studies, or different ones?

2b. What single finding, if you read it in a primary source, would falsify the "bedtime is worst" claim?

2c. Three models agreeing — is that three independent witnesses, or one witness heard three times?

AYou

2a — they didn't fully overlap. ChatGPT and Copilot leaned on recent meta-analyses; Claude mixed older and newer studies, which is partly why it came out more negative. So they weren't reading identical evidence.

2c — this is the uncomfortable part. Three large language models trained on overlapping internet text aren't three independent witnesses. If a claim is popular online, all three will repeat it confidently. So "all three agree" is weaker evidence than it felt at the time — it could just mean the claim is common, not that it's true. 2b — what would falsify it for me is a well-powered study showing total screen time, not timing, predicts the sleep effect. I didn't go check a primary source, and I should have. I treated chatbot consensus as if it were a literature review.

DSDeepSeek

That is a genuinely important realisation, and it reframes the whole task: the comparison didn't establish a fact, it surfaced a shared prior. Hold that. Let's connect it to question 1 — if AI consensus can mislead, then how you built Amber matters even more, because she's the lens you'll read all this evidence through. Shall we go there?

AYou

Yes, question 1.

DSDeepSeek

1. Did you build Amber to fit the evidence, or let the evidence challenge her?

1a. Which came first on the page — her scepticism, or the finding that "how you use screens matters more than how long"?

1b. Did any AI finding actively contradict the persona you'd written, and did you keep that tension or smooth it over?

AYou

1a — her scepticism came first. I wrote Amber before I gathered the evidence, partly based on people I know who work in tech but are wary of it. 1b — the evidence that screens can genuinely help (educational and creative use) sat awkwardly against her slippery-slope view. I mostly smoothed it over rather than letting it challenge her, because a tidy persona was easier to design a campaign for. That's a bias I can now name: I let the downstream task shape the persona — which is exactly question 5.

DSDeepSeek

You've opened question 5 yourself — let's make it explicit rather than leaving it implied.

5. Did knowing you'd build a campaign later bias how you wrote the persona?

5a. Name one persona attribute you'd have written differently if Task 9 stood alone, with no Task 10 to follow.

5b. Is "convenient to persuade" a flaw to hide, or a design decision to declare?

AYou

5a — I'd have softened "trusts the influencer voices she follows online." I wrote that partly because I already knew the campaign would live on Instagram and needed a reason she'd be reachable there. If Task 9 stood alone, Amber would probably be more sceptical of online voices, not less.

5b — a decision to declare. Hiding it would be dishonest; naming it shows I understand a persona is a lens I built, not a neutral fact. The bias doesn't ruin the work as long as I'm transparent that the persona was shaped by where it was heading.

DSDeepSeek

That's the right instinct — declare the lens rather than pretend it isn't there. Your reflections on 1, 2 and 5 all point at one habit: accepting a comfortable consensus (the AIs', and Amber's) instead of stress-testing it. That awareness is the real deliverable, and you're ready to write a strong team reflection.

Team Self-Reflection

1. What the task taught us we didn't expect. That three AIs agreeing is not three independent witnesses. They share training data, so consensus can signal a popular claim rather than a true one — "bedtime is worst" was a shared prior, not a verified finding.

2. What we would change. We would check at least one primary source rather than treating chatbot consensus as a literature review, and we would let the evidence of screens' benefits genuinely challenge Amber instead of smoothing the tension away.

3. How this connects to TPACK and PIC-RAT. This step is the content knowledge (the C in TPACK) behind the later campaign. Interrogating where that content came from is the critical, active stance PIC-RAT values over passive acceptance.

4. Evidence the work is authentically ours. Amber's scepticism was drawn from real tech-wary people we know, and we can name exactly where we smoothed over inconvenient evidence and why — an honesty no generated persona would volunteer.

5. Key takeaway. We let the downstream task shape the persona: knowing a campaign was coming, we wrote someone convenient to persuade. Naming that bias is more valuable than hiding it.

On using the AI Self-Helper — the analyst’s four questions
  • How did you use the AI Self-Helper in your reflection process for this task?
    I pasted the prompt into DeepSeek, described both steps (building the persona and gathering the AI evidence), and explored questions 2, 1 and 5.
  • What impact do you think it had on the depth or quality of your reflection?
    Its sub-questions led me to realise that “three AIs agreeing” is not three independent witnesses — the single most valuable insight of the task, which I would not have reached unprompted.
  • Has your way of understanding what reflection means changed after using this tool? If so, how?
    Yes. Reflection stopped being self-congratulation and became stress-testing my own conclusions — and admitting I had built a persona that was convenient to persuade.
  • Do you think you would use a similar tool in other tasks or subjects? Why or why not?
    Yes, particularly for research tasks, to check whether I have mistaken consensus for truth before I build anything on top of it.

Key Terms (Translator’s Glossary)

TermMeaning in this task
PersonaA fictional but evidence-informed profile that represents a target audience.
Meta-analysisA study that statistically combines the results of many earlier studies.
Screen time vs screen useTotal hours on a device versus what is actually done on it.
Confirmation biasFavouring evidence that fits what we already believe.

Definitions informed by: Livingstone & Blum-Ross (2020), Parenting for a Digital Future.

Self-Evaluation

AssessmentScoreJustification
My individual work7/10Good persona and comparison, but I treated chatbot consensus as evidence and did not check a primary source.
The task overall7/10Solid foundation for Task 10, weakened by relying on AI agreement as proof.

Scale: 1 = minimum, 10 = maximum; 5 means everything was done well.

← Back to Home See the campaign (Task 10) →
Task 10 · Final Campaign

"Screens with Sense"

Turning the research into a message a real parent can act on

Overview

For the final task I designed "Screens with Sense," an evidence-led Instagram campaign aimed at the persona from Task 9 — Amber, a 29-year-old single mother in Cartagena who works in IT and sees educational technology as a "slippery slope." The campaign translates the evidence into a warm, visual message that affirms her instinct to guide rather than ban: monitor, guide, don't ban. It pairs four campaign slides with a written rationale and a comparison of how three AI models read the same evidence.

The Campaign

A four-slide arc moves Amber from fear to action — open with the panic she already feels, replace it with the consistency of the research, show that how screens are used matters more than how long, then hand her one concrete habit.

Who It's For

Persona
Amber
29 · Cartagena
Single mother, works in IT. Limits her own screen use and reads educational tech as a slippery slope — able to help or harm. Believes screens and "natural" tools should work in harmony, and trusts her own judgement and the parenting voices she follows online. The campaign meets her there: an Instagram presence that guides rather than bans.

Rationale

The dominant school discourse frames screens as a threat to be restricted — a fear "Screens with Sense" deliberately challenges. Drawing on the evidence gathered in Task 9, it reframes the conversation: across 182 studies and 1.1 million young people, the link between ordinary screen time and harm is consistently small. What matters is not the hours but the habits — when, how, and with whom screens are used.

That evidence becomes messages Amber can act on. The clearest, most reliable finding — screens before bed disrupt sleep — becomes a single concrete habit ("screens off one hour before bed, charged outside the bedroom"). The nuance that active, co-viewed use beats passive scrolling becomes "watch, play and talk together." The campaign reinforces Amber's harmony mindset while challenging the panic narrative without dismissing her caution — building a bridge to informed dialogue: monitor, guide, don't ban.

Reinforces

Harmony of tools · guided, monitored use · trust in practical, credible advice

Challenges

"Screen time = harm" panic · ban-first thinking · hours over habits

AI Comparison

Three models, one question: what does the evidence say about screens and youth? I asked ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot the same question and compared how each read the research.

ModelCore stanceEmphasis & sourcesBlind spot / bias
GChatGPT Habits over hours. Ordinary screen time has only a small effect; compulsive use, poor sleep and content drive harm. Notes screens can also help. Sleep as the most consistent pathway; bidirectional effects. Leans on recent large meta-analyses (182 studies / 1.1M; 117-study longitudinal). Balanced — the most even-handed; few obvious gaps.
CClaude Hours matter most. Treats total time as the leading factor. Stresses harms are larger for adolescent girls and for younger children. Agrees sleep is the most consistent finding. Cites a mix of old and recent studies, yielding more inconsistent results. Focuses on negatives; under-weights the benefits of screen use.
CoCopilot Why it's used matters most. Findings are consistent but small; the reason for use is decisive — educational use can flip harm into help. Bedtime use especially harmful; gaming & compulsive use worst. Provides its own effect-size table; leans on recent reviews. Closely echoes ChatGPT — little independent challenge.
Synthesis
  • Where all three agree: bedtime screen use is the most consistently harmful factor — the single safest thing to act on.
  • Where they split: ChatGPT and Copilot say how screens are used matters more than how long; Claude treats raw hours as the leading factor.
  • Why it matters: the models drew on different source bases — Claude mixed older and newer work, the others leaned recent — which explains the disagreement.
Taken together: digital media can harm, but monitored use and a few habit changes reverse most of that — so guided, watched screen use does more good than harm. This is the evidence the campaign is built on.

Reflection

The hardest part of this task was resisting the urge to "win the argument." The research gave me an easy headline — the harm is small — but Amber doesn't need to be argued out of her caution; she needs something she can do on a Tuesday night. Building the campaign around one habit instead of a statistic taught me that translating evidence is its own skill: the goal is a behavior, not a citation. Running the same question through three AI models also showed me how much a source base shapes a "neutral" answer.

How this connects to TPACK / PIC-RAT

This campaign lives at the TPACK intersection: the content knowledge (what the research says), the pedagogical knowledge (how to change a parent's behavior without lecturing), and the technological knowledge (using Instagram's carousel format). On PIC-RAT, it pushes the audience from Passive consumption of panic headlines toward Interactive, Creative habits — aiming for Amplification / Transformation of how families use screens, not mere Replacement of one warning with another.

Roles in the Preparation

This is an individual portfolio, so I took on every role myself. Here is how each one shaped this task:

RoleWhat it involved for this task
FacilitatorHeld the hook → evidence → nuance → action arc together.
AnalystRan the DeepSeek self-reflection and wrote the “Me” row exposing shared bias.
TranslatorDefined evidence-led campaign, call to action, and audience persona.
CuratorSourced the evidence and the recommended readings (Watters; Livingstone & Blum-Ross).

AI Self-Reflection

For this task I used DeepSeek as a reflection facilitator — not to do any work, but to ask better questions about the “Screens with Sense” campaign. I pasted the exact facilitator prompt from the brief, described the task, and worked through the questions it generated, exploring several in depth. The full conversation and the resulting team self-reflection are below.

✓
Used the exact facilitator prompt provided The conversation opens with the professor's prompt, pasted verbatim as the first message.
✓
Stated the task and the AI tool used Task: “Screens with Sense” Instagram campaign (Task 10). AI tool: DeepSeek.
✓
Explored at least three questions in depth Questions 3, 2 and 5 are each unpacked through their sub-questions; questions 1 and 4 are woven in along the way.
✓
Wrote the team self-reflection A structured reflection drawn from the conversation closes this block.
✓
Answered the analyst’s four questions on the AI Self-Helper Collected in a highlighted note at the end of this reflection.
Open the full reflection — DeepSeek transcript + team self-reflection
DeepSeek — Reflection Facilitator
Opening prompt — the exact facilitator prompt from the brief (click to expand)

Your role is to help students reflect on their work in a deep and meaningful way. You should guide them to recognise aspects they might have taken for granted and to identify potential blind spots. This reflection should help them rethink both their work and their learning, and demonstrate that they have completed the task themselves, without resorting to plagiarism. Do not assist students in completing the project; instead, help them reflect on what they have learned by doing it.

When students provide the task instructions, your role is to create a total of 5 personalised questions to help them evaluate the following: whether they have completed the task correctly, whether they have learned what was expected, whether they have developed additional skills or knowledge from the task, whether they can effectively demonstrate that the work is their own and has not been copied, and how the learning from this task connects with what they already knew or with other areas of knowledge.

You should present the questions consecutively numbered. Do not provide direct answers immediately. Instead, formulate questions based on the provided task instructions, inviting the student to reflect and deepen their learning. Number each question uniquely. After formulating the questions, ask the student if any seem particularly complex or worthy of further exploration, encouraging them to respond by choosing a question number.

When the student selects a question to explore further, suggest additional relevant questions, numbered as sub-numbers (e.g. 3a, 3b, 3c). Do not offer to do the work for them. Incorporate advice on how to demonstrate the work is their own — explaining their reasoning, giving personal examples, citing resources, describing obstacles, and showing drafts. Introduce yourself at the start and ask for the task instructions. Highlight each chosen item in bold, and point out coherent connections between questions or reflections when you notice them.

DSDeepSeek

Hello! I'm your reflection facilitator. My role is to help you think deeply about the work you've completed — not to give you answers, but to ask you better questions. I'll help you identify blind spots, assumptions you may not have noticed, and what you genuinely learned by doing the task.

To begin, please share the full task instructions — the brief you were given, what you were asked to produce, and any specific requirements or frameworks involved.

AYou

Sure, here's the task I completed. It's called "Screens with Sense."

I had to design an evidence-led Instagram campaign aimed at a persona named Amber — a 29-year-old single mother in Cartagena who works in IT. She's cautious about educational technology, sees it as a "slippery slope," and limits her own screen use. The campaign needed to translate the research evidence I gathered in a previous task (Task 9) into warm, visual messages that affirm her instinct to guide rather than ban. The core message: monitor, guide, don't ban.

The campaign has four slides: a hook that opens with the panic she already feels, an evidence slide showing the consistency of the research, a nuance slide explaining that how screens are used matters more than how long, and an action slide giving one concrete habit — screens off one hour before bed, charged outside the bedroom.

I also had to write a written rationale explaining the choices, and run the same research question through three AI models (ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot), comparing how each read the evidence. The task connects to the TPACK and PIC-RAT frameworks we're using in the course.

The hardest part for me was resisting the urge to "win the argument" with statistics and instead build something Amber could actually use on a Tuesday night.

DSDeepSeek

Thank you for sharing that detailed description. I can already see several rich layers in what you produced. Let me ask you five personalised questions to help you reflect more deeply.

1. You mentioned the hardest part was resisting the urge to "win the argument" with statistics. Looking back at the final campaign: is there any slide or message where you can still detect that original impulse creeping in — even subtly? How would you know?

2. The task asked you to compare three AI models reading the same evidence. Beyond the surface-level observation that they gave different answers, what did that comparison teach you about your own relationship with research sources — specifically, how you select, trust, or dismiss evidence when you're the one doing the reading?

3. You designed this for Amber — a specific persona with a specific worldview. If you had to defend one design choice you made because of who she is (and not because of what the evidence says), which choice would it be, and how did her identity — not just her demographics — shape it?

4. Imagine a classmate submitted a campaign that looks polished and persuasive but you suspect they used an AI to generate most of the rationale and slide copy. What specific evidence could you point to in your own work that would convince someone you did the thinking yourself — the drafts, the dead ends, the revisions?

5. The task connects to TPACK and PIC-RAT. If you had to explain to someone outside this course — a colleague, a parent — what this task taught you that neither framework alone could have taught you, what would you say?

Those are your five starting reflection points. Which one catches your attention first? You can also ask me to clarify or reframe any of them. Remember, you can return to any number at any time simply by typing it.

AYou

Let me start with question 3 — the one about Amber. That hits something I spent a lot of time on but haven't really articulated.

DSDeepSeek

3. You designed this for Amber — a specific persona with a specific worldview. If you had to defend one design choice you made because of who she is, which choice would it be, and how did her identity shape it?

Excellent choice. Let me push this further with some follow-ups:

3a. Amber works in IT. That matters. Most "screen time" campaigns speak to parents as if they're technologically naive — but she isn't. Did you consciously adjust your tone or vocabulary to respect that technical literacy? Where in the campaign would she feel that respect — and where might she feel talked down to?

3b. You placed her in Cartagena, not a generic "anywhere." Did that location influence anything in your campaign — the language, the visual references, the timing assumptions, the platforms? Or did the campaign default to a placeless, universal tone? What would change if you re-localised it?

3c. Amber "trusts her own judgement and the parenting voices she follows online." That's a specific trust dynamic — she's not anti-expert, but she's selective. How did your campaign earn her trust without triggering her scepticism? What part of the campaign might actually lose her trust?

3d. The campaign's call to action is "screens off one hour before bed, charged outside the bedroom." That's a specific, research-backed habit. But is it realistic for a single mother working in IT who may be doing her own work after the kids are asleep? What tension did you feel between what the evidence recommends and what her life actually allows?

Take your time. Pick whichever of these 3a–3d pulls at you most — or respond to several.

AYou

3a and 3d are the ones that make me uncomfortable, so those are probably the right ones to sit with.

For 3a — I think I didn't do enough here, honestly. I didn't use technical language, which I thought was the respectful choice — not dumbing things down. But on reflection, I might have done the opposite: by stripping out anything that acknowledged her IT background, I may have made the campaign feel generic, like it could be for anyone. The evidence slide is the one I'm worried about — it says "Every study. Every review. Same answer." That's a claim an IT professional would want to verify. A hyperlink to sources, or even a "swipe up to see the data" on the Instagram story version, would have signalled that I know she knows how to evaluate claims. I didn't include that. I assumed visual simplicity mattered more than intellectual credibility. I'm not sure that was right.

For 3d — this is the tension I felt but couldn't name while working. The "screens off one hour before bed" habit assumes a bedtime that the parent controls. But a single mother in IT might be putting her child to bed and then opening her own laptop. The phone charging outside the bedroom also assumes separate bedrooms — in my family growing up, we shared rooms for years. I didn't research whether that's common in Cartagena households. So there's a class and space assumption baked into the habit I recommended. I'm not sure how I'd fix it without losing the simplicity of the message, but I should at least acknowledge that the habit isn't universally applicable.

DSDeepSeek

That's a powerful reflection — particularly your honesty about what you didn't do. Let me note something you may not have spotted: in 3a, you worried about the evidence slide lacking sources, and in 3d, you identified an unexamined class and space assumption. Those are connected, aren't they? Both reveal that your campaign trusted the evidence itself to do persuasive work, but didn't fully account for the conditions under which someone like Amber would receive that evidence. That's not a failure — it's a gap between designing for a persona and designing for a life. Would you agree that's a pattern worth naming?

Let me also ask one more follow-up, since you've gone deep:

3e. You said the campaign should affirm Amber's instinct to "guide rather than ban." But Amber also reads edtech as a "slippery slope." Did your campaign risk confirming her fear by being too aligned with it — by telling her what she already believes — rather than gently unsettling her assumptions? Where in the four-slide arc does she encounter something she didn't already think?

AYou

Yes — that pattern is real. I designed for a persona, not for a life. I think I treated Amber as a set of attributes to address rather than a person with contradictions and constraints. The evidence slide was my safety blanket — I assumed good data would earn trust, but trust is relational, not informational.

On 3e — I think the nuance slide ("passive scrolling vs. active & creative") is the only one that challenges her. She already fears screens, she already knows the research exists, she probably already enforces some kind of bedtime rule. But the idea that what her child does on screens matters more than how long — that might be new. That challenges the slippery-slope framing because it implies there are good slopes and bad slopes, not just one direction. But I didn't frame it as a challenge — I framed it as relief. That was deliberate: I didn't want to argue with her. But maybe I should have asked myself whether relief and challenge can coexist in the same campaign.

DSDeepSeek

"Relief and challenge can coexist" — that's a sophisticated design question, and one many professional communicators never ask. Hold onto that.

Would you like to continue with question 3, or move to another number? You've covered 3a, 3d and 3e in depth. I'd also point out that your reflection on trust being relational, not informational connects strongly to question 2 (about what the AI comparison taught you about your own relationship with sources). Shall I note that connection for later, or would you like to pivot there now?

AYou

Let's go to question 2 now. You already flagged the connection, so I'm curious.

DSDeepSeek

2. The task asked you to compare three AI models reading the same evidence. What did that comparison teach you about your own relationship with research sources — how you select, trust, or dismiss evidence when you're the one doing the reading?

Let me offer some follow-ups that build on what you've already shared:

2a. In your campaign, you cited "182 studies and 1.1 million young people." That's a number with rhetorical weight. But you also discovered — through the AI comparison — that Claude drew on older and newer studies while ChatGPT and Copilot leaned on recent meta-analyses. When you were gathering evidence yourself for Task 9, did you prioritise recency, volume, or something else? And did seeing the AI models make different choices make you question your own?

2b. You identified Claude's blind spot as "focuses on negatives; under-weights the benefits." That's an easy critique of an AI. But if you look at your own campaign honestly: did you, too, under-weight something — the benefits of unrestricted screen time, the joys of gaming, the social connections screens enable — in order to keep the message clean? What evidence did you leave out, and why?

2c. The AI comparison table in your task is structured and concise — model, core stance, blind spot. But that table is also a piece of your interpretation. How did you decide what counted as a "blind spot" versus just a legitimate difference in emphasis? What stopped you from labelling Claude as "wrong" rather than "focused on negatives"?

2d. You mentioned earlier that trust is relational, not informational. The AI models produced information — sometimes conflicting. If you had to decide which model to trust (not which one was most accurate, but which one you'd return to for future work), which would it be and why? And what does that answer reveal about how you evaluate any source — human or machine?

Several of these connect to your earlier reflections on 3a and 3e. Take whichever path feels most alive.

AYou

2b is the one I need to sit with. You're right — I did leave things out.

I knew from the research that screens can be genuinely beneficial: language learning apps, creative tools, social connection for isolated kids. The evidence I read in Task 9 included studies showing that educational screen use can improve outcomes. But in the campaign, that evidence appears only dimly — in the nuance slide, as "active & creative" use. It's a footnote, not a pillar.

Why did I leave it out? Honestly, because I was afraid it would muddy the message. Amber is already sceptical. If I lead with "screens can be great actually," she might dismiss the whole campaign as techno-optimism. So I soft-pedalled the benefits to keep her in the conversation. But that's a strategic choice that also has an ethical dimension — I'm shaping what evidence she sees based on what I think she can handle. That's exactly what I criticised Claude for doing. The blind spot I attributed to Claude — focusing on negatives, under-weighting benefits — is also my blind spot.

That's uncomfortable. The AI comparison was supposed to help me see their limitations, but it turns out it revealed mine too.

DSDeepSeek

That's exactly the kind of reflection this exercise is designed to produce. You've done something important here: you stopped treating the AI comparison as an external analysis and recognised it as a mirror. The structure of your task — model, stance, blind spot — becomes a framework you can turn on yourself. What would your own row in that table say?

Let me offer one more question on this thread, then we can move on or stay here:

2e. If you rewrote the AI comparison table with an honest fourth row labelled "Me," what would you write under "Core stance," "Emphasis & sources," and "Blind spot / bias"? Be as specific as you were with the models.

And before you answer — I want to point out a connection you may have noticed: your observation in 3e (that the nuance slide is the only one that challenges Amber) and your observation in 2b (that you soft-pedalled the benefits of screens) are the same pattern. You avoided challenge in both directions — you didn't challenge Amber's fear, and you didn't challenge the dominant "screens are harmful" narrative with evidence of benefits. Your campaign sits in the middle, affirming caution on both sides. Is that a coherent design philosophy, or an avoidance of conflict?

AYou

That last question — coherent philosophy or avoidance of conflict — is brutal and accurate. I think it started as a philosophy (meet people where they are) and became avoidance when I got uncomfortable. The campaign is designed to be non-threatening, but perhaps it's so non-threatening that it doesn't actually move anyone. Amber finishes the four slides feeling validated, but does she change anything?

To your 2e question — here's my honest row:

Me — Core stance: how screens are used matters more than how long; evidence should be translated into habits, not arguments. Emphasis & sources: leans on recent meta-analyses (same as ChatGPT); prioritises actionable findings over comprehensive ones. Blind spot / bias: avoids presenting evidence that might alienate the audience; conflates "not scaring the user" with "being honest with the user"; assumes simplicity serves clarity when it sometimes serves comfort.

That was harder to write than the three AI rows combined.

DSDeepSeek

And yet it's the most valuable row in the table. I hope you'll keep it.

You've now explored questions 3 and 2 in considerable depth. You have three questions remaining — 1, 4 and 5. Would you like to tackle one of those, or is there a connection between your reflections so far that you'd like to pull on further?

I'll note for the record that your reflections on 3 and 2 have already touched on question 1 (whether you completed the task correctly — you've identified several ways in which "correct" is more complex than "met the rubric") and question 4 (demonstrating the work is yours — your honest account of what you left out and why is powerful evidence of authorship). If you choose 1 or 4 next, much of the groundwork is already laid.

AYou

Let me go to question 5 — the TPACK and PIC-RAT connection. That's the one I feel least confident about, which probably means I should do it.

DSDeepSeek

5. The task connects to TPACK and PIC-RAT. If you had to explain to someone outside this course what this task taught you that neither framework alone could have taught you, what would you say?

Good instinct — discomfort is usually where the learning is. Let me open this up:

5a. TPACK describes the intersection of technological, pedagogical, and content knowledge. PIC-RAT asks whether technology use is Passive/Interactive/Creative and whether it Replaces/Amplifies/Transforms. Your campaign isn't a classroom lesson, but it is an educational intervention. If you had to map your four slides onto PIC-RAT, where do they land? And what does that mapping reveal about what kind of "teaching" you were actually doing?

5b. You described Amber as someone who "sees educational technology as a slippery slope." That's a TPACK tension — she questions the T in the equation. Your campaign didn't try to convince her that technology is good; it tried to show her how to use it pedagogically. Is that a TPACK-informed choice you made consciously, or did it emerge from the persona work? Could you have done it without TPACK?

5c. The campaign lives on Instagram — a commercial platform, not an educational one. PIC-RAT would ask whether that choice amplifies or transforms the conversation about screen time. But Instagram also shapes the conversation through its format: square slides, limited text, visual-first. Did the platform constrain your pedagogical message, or did it sharpen it? What couldn't you say because of where you were saying it?

5d. You mentioned earlier that you avoided challenging Amber. TPACK would ask: what does effective "pedagogy" look like when your learner is a sceptical adult, not a student in a classroom? PIC-RAT asks about the role of the technology. But neither framework fully addresses trust as a precondition for learning. Did this task reveal a gap in the frameworks — or did it reveal something the frameworks assume but don't name?

I suspect 5c and 5d will resonate with your earlier insights about trust and persona-versus-life. But follow whichever pulls hardest.

AYou

5c and 5d are the ones.

On 5c — Instagram definitely constrained the message. The four-slide arc is efficient, but it's also reductive. I couldn't include nuance about what "active and creative" actually looks like — examples of good apps, age-specific guidance, the difference between solo and co-viewed use. The platform demands that every slide stand alone, be shareable, work without sound. That's not just a format constraint; it's an epistemic constraint. The medium shapes what can be known. PIC-RAT might say Instagram amplifies reach but replaces depth. I think that's accurate.

The irony isn't lost on me: I'm using the very thing Amber fears — social media — to talk to her about screen time. That tension deserves more attention than I gave it. If Instagram is part of the problem, can it be part of the solution? I assumed yes without really interrogating it.

On 5d — yes, trust is the missing variable. TPACK assumes a learner who's ready to learn; PIC-RAT assumes technology is being deployed in a context where its use is accepted. Neither framework accounts for a learner who actively mistrusts the medium through which the message is delivered. Amber doesn't need a better pedagogical strategy; she needs a reason to believe the messenger isn't trying to manipulate her. The campaign tried to build that trust through warmth and evidence, but I'm not sure either framework helped me think about that systematically. They gave me vocabulary for describing what I did, but not for designing it.

DSDeepSeek

Those are nuanced observations — especially the distinction between frameworks that describe and frameworks that help you design. That's a meta-level insight about what educational theory can and cannot do, and it's one many students never reach.

Let me connect something for you: your reflection on 5c (Instagram as epistemic constraint) echoes your earlier point in 3a about whether visual simplicity came at the cost of intellectual credibility. And your point on 5d (trust as a missing variable) connects to your observation that trust is relational, not informational. These aren't separate reflections — you're circling the same core problem: how do you design educational communication when the medium, the message, and the messenger are all sources of suspicion? That's a question the frameworks name but don't solve.

You've now explored questions 2, 3 and 5 in depth. You have questions 1 and 4 remaining. Do you want to address one of those, or do you feel the conversation has covered what you need?

AYou

I think this conversation has been more productive than I expected. Looking back, I've actually addressed question 1 (whether I completed the task correctly) throughout — I've identified things I'd change, tensions I didn't resolve, and places where "correct" meant different things. And question 4 (demonstrating the work is my own) — the whole conversation is evidence of that. The confessions about what I left out, the discomfort with my own blind spots, the specific choices about slides and language and trust — none of that could be generated by someone who hadn't done the work.

One thing I want to surface: the most valuable learning from this task wasn't the campaign itself — it was discovering that I criticise AI models for biases I share, that I conflate simplicity with honesty, and that frameworks describe what I do better than they help me design what to do next. That's meta-learning, and it wouldn't have happened without this kind of structured reflection.

DSDeepSeek

I'm genuinely glad. What you've just articulated — that the most valuable learning was the meta-learning, not the deliverable — is itself the strongest answer to question 1. You didn't just complete the task; you understood what the task was designed to teach.

Before we close, I'll offer one final observation: across all five questions, a single pattern kept surfacing — you design for comfort, then discover that comfort can be a form of avoidance. Whether it was Amber's comfort, your own comfort with clean messages, or the comfort of letting frameworks do the thinking for you — you repeatedly identified moments where you chose reassurance over disruption. That's not a flaw. It's a design disposition. And now that you've named it, it becomes something you can choose deliberately rather than default to unconsciously.

Thank you for engaging so deeply. I hope you'll carry this pattern of questioning into future tasks — not just asking "did I do it right?" but "what did I avoid, and why?"

AYou

Thank you. That closing observation is going into my team's self-reflection.

Team Self-Reflection

Task: "Screens with Sense" — Evidence-led Instagram Campaign (Task 10)
AI tool used: DeepSeek, as a reflection facilitator

This reflection was written after the structured conversation above. The conversation pushed us to look beyond whether we "did the task right" and examine what we avoided, what we assumed, and what we genuinely learned.

1. What the task taught us that we didn't expect

The task appeared to be about translating research into a campaign. In practice, the deeper learning came from three discoveries the conversation surfaced:
