  • We criticise AI models for biases we ourselves share. The AI comparison table was meant to reveal how ChatGPT, Claude and Copilot each had blind spots — but writing an honest fourth row for ourselves showed the same pattern: selectively presenting evidence to keep the audience comfortable rather than informed. This mirror effect was the most uncomfortable and most valuable insight.
  • Designing for a persona is not the same as designing for a life. The campaign treated Amber as a set of attributes (IT worker, single mother, sceptical) but failed to account for material constraints — shared bedrooms in Cartagena, a parent who works after the child sleeps. The habit we recommended assumed conditions we didn't verify.
  • Frameworks describe better than they help design. TPACK and PIC-RAT gave us vocabulary to label what we did, but neither addressed trust as a precondition for learning — something our persona work revealed as central.

2. What we would change

  • Include verifiable source links on the evidence slide, respecting Amber's technical literacy rather than prioritising visual simplicity over intellectual credibility.
  • Acknowledge the limitations of the bedtime-screen habit explicitly ("this may look different in your household") rather than presenting it as universal.
  • Give equal weight to the benefits of active screen use, rather than relegating it to a single nuance slide. The evidence supports it, and withholding it is ethically questionable — even if strategically convenient.
  • Interrogate the platform choice (Instagram) more honestly, asking whether using a medium Amber mistrusts to deliver a message about screen time is coherent or contradictory.

3. How this connects to TPACK and PIC-RAT

  • TPACK helped us recognise that Amber's scepticism is a Content–Technology tension: she accepts the pedagogical goal (guiding her child) but questions the technological means (screens). The campaign bridged that gap not by arguing for technology but by showing pedagogical use of it — a TPACK-informed move.
  • PIC-RAT helped us see that Instagram as a platform amplifies reach but replaces depth. The format (square slides, no sound, standalone messages) shaped what evidence we could present as much as any pedagogical decision we made.
  • However, neither framework accounted for trust or platform scepticism as variables. This task revealed that educational design in real-world contexts requires attending to relational dynamics that sit outside both models.

4. Evidence that the work is authentically ours

  • The rationale was developed through iterative drafting — early versions tried to "win the argument" with statistics before we deliberately pivoted to habit-centred messaging.
  • The AI comparison required us to formulate the query, evaluate three separate model responses, identify differences in source bases, and synthesise our own interpretation. No AI generated that analysis for us.
  • The design choices (hook–evidence–nuance–action arc; the specific habit selection; the decision to affirm rather than challenge) were debated within the team and traceable to specific evidence from Task 9 and specific persona attributes.
  • The blind spots we identified (class and space assumptions, soft-pedalling benefits, platform irony) are not things we could have named without doing the work and then reflecting on it critically.

5. Key takeaway

The most important thing this task taught us is that translating evidence into practice is not just about accuracy — it's about honesty. Simplifying evidence for an audience can easily become suppressing evidence for comfort. The line between "making it accessible" and "making it palatable" is thinner than we thought. Naming that tension — and sitting with it rather than resolving it — was the real learning.

On using the AI Self-Helper — the analyst’s four questions
  • How did you use the AI Self-Helper in your reflection process for this task?
    I pasted the prompt into DeepSeek, described the “Screens with Sense” campaign, and explored questions 3, 2 and 5 across many sub-questions.
  • What impact do you think it had on the depth or quality of your reflection?
    It turned the AI comparison into a mirror: writing an honest “Me” row exposed that I share the biases I had criticised in the models. The depth went well beyond a normal write-up.
  • Has your way of understanding what reflection means changed after using this tool? If so, how?
    Yes. Reflection became about naming avoidance — designing for comfort instead of disruption — rather than defending the choices I had made.
  • Do you think you would use a similar tool in other tasks or subjects? Why or why not?
    Yes, for any design or persuasion task, because it surfaces the gap between designing for a persona and designing for a life.

Key Terms (Translator’s Glossary)

TermMeaning in this task
Evidence-led campaignCommunication whose claims are grounded in research.
Call to action (CTA)The single concrete step the audience is asked to take.
Audience personaThe specific person a message is designed for.
PIC-RATUsed here to judge the campaign’s level of learner engagement and impact.

Definitions informed by: Watters (2015); Livingstone & Blum-Ross (2020).

Self-Evaluation

AssessmentScoreJustification
My individual work9/10The campaign and the “mirror” reflection were my best work; the small gap is the missing source links on the evidence slide.
The task overall9/10Coherent, honest, and well-grounded — my strongest activity.

Scale: 1 = minimum, 10 = maximum; 5 means everything was done well.

← Back to Home
DataBox

The Currency of Everything

Reviewing an immersive VR game built to teach data literacy

Overview

DataBox is an interactive virtual-reality video game developed by researchers at Slate in collaboration with the Dalai Project. Its goal is to break a common misconception — that "data" means numbers or advanced coding meant only for professionals — by walking players through the full life cycle of data. The game's premise is that data is pervasive in everyday life: it is the "currency of everything that surrounds us." I reviewed it as a ready-made educational resource and assessed it against the same frameworks that anchor this portfolio.

The two halves of the game in one view: why data literacy matters (left) and how DataBox builds it (right), with the player and Databot at the centre.

Watch my short video

Can’t see the player? Open the video in Google Drive ↗

The Data Literacy Gap

DataBox is built around the distance between what data actually is and what most people think it is — the gap the game sets out to close.

The Reality

Data surrounds our daily life. It is the currency of everything — woven into routines, choices, and the services we use, not confined to spreadsheets.

The Misconception

The public believes data is just advanced coding terminology or numbers meant only for professionals. Most people cannot even define it.

Why It Matters — The Threat of Illiteracy

The inability to read and evaluate data isn't neutral; the game frames it as a real risk with two faces:

Vulnerability to Misinformation

Without the tools to gauge whether data is reliable or biased, users are easily misled.

Privacy Risks

Users who don't understand how their personal data is extracted and handled can't protect it. DataBox exposes that handling so players become critical thinkers, not easy targets.

Inside the Game

The PedigreeDeveloped by researchers at Slate in collaboration with the Dalai Project.
The MediumAn immersive, interactive virtual-reality video game — players board a virtual spaceship.
The GuideDatabot, an automated character who explains factual information about data and lets players interact with the environment to make abstract ideas concrete.
The MissionBreak the "just numbers" misconception by walking players step-by-step through the complete life cycle of data.

Aligned to the DigComp Framework

The game maps directly onto two competences of the European Digital Competence Framework (DigComp), and the payoff of both is the same: critical thinkers.

DigComp competenceWhat DataBox teaches
1Information & Data Literacy Players learn to identify where data comes from, evaluate whether it is reliable, and spot inherent bias.
4Safety Players see exactly how personal data is handled and processed in the real world — making them less susceptible to privacy risks and misinformation.

The Pedagogical Loop

DataBox uses a self-paced, fail-safe learning cycle. Crucially, there is no penalty for mistakes — wrong answers become teaching moments rather than failures.

How a player learns
  • 1 · Board the spaceship: enter the immersive environment.
  • 2 · Meet Databot: engage the automated guide to learn factual data concepts.
  • 3 · Interact & be assessed: complete hands-on tasks, then answer questions to test the concept just learned.
  • 4 · Elaborate feedback: on a wrong answer, Databot explains why the option is incorrect — so the mistake becomes part of the learning.
The fail-safe loop lets players master concepts entirely at their own pace, turning a quiz into a conversation.

Frictionless Accessibility

A VR game could easily be the least accessible resource in a classroom. DataBox deliberately removes the usual barriers:

BarrierTraditional ed-tech VRDataBox
PlatformRequires heavy software downloads.100% browser-based — no downloads needed.
HardwareExpensive VR headsets are mandatory.Headset optional; fully playable with a standard mouse and monitor.

Zero barriers to entry — accessible for any classroom, on any device.

My Evaluation

Run through the course's two lenses, DataBox holds up well. In PIC-RAT terms it isn't a passive replacement for a worksheet: players act on the environment and get adaptive feedback, placing it firmly at the Interactive end and pushing toward Transformation — it teaches something (the lived reality of the data life cycle) that would be hard to deliver any other way. In TPACK terms its strength is the tight fit between content (data literacy & safety), pedagogy (a no-penalty feedback loop), and technology (frictionless, browser-based VR). My one reservation is assessment depth: short knowledge-check questions risk measuring recall more than transfer, so I'd pair the game with a follow-up discussion to be sure the critical-thinking goal actually lands.

How this connects to the frameworks & the rest of the portfolio

DataBox is the portfolio's clearest example of evaluating an existing resource rather than building one. It echoes Task 7's focus on the ethics and safety of technology for learners, and it is assessed with the same TPACK / PIC-RAT lenses introduced in Task 1 — the through-line of every task here.

Roles in the Preparation

This is an individual portfolio, so I took on every role myself. Here is how each one shaped this task:

RoleWhat it involved for this task
FacilitatorFramed the self-set review explicitly against TPACK and PIC-RAT.
AnalystRan the Copilot self-reflection and separated verifiable facts from marketing.
TranslatorDefined data literacy, DigComp, gamification, and the data life cycle.
CuratorSourced the game’s materials and credited NotebookLM for the infographic.

AI Self-Reflection

For the DataBox review I used Copilot as a reflection facilitator to check whether I evaluated the game critically or just relayed its marketing. I pasted the facilitator prompt from the brief, described the self-set review task, and explored the questions it raised.

✓
Used the exact facilitator prompt provided The conversation opens with the professor's prompt, pasted verbatim as the first message.
✓
Stated the task and the AI tool used Task: DataBox — review of a VR data-literacy game. AI tool: Copilot.
✓
Explored at least three questions in depth Questions 2, 4 and 5 are unpacked through their sub-questions; 1 and 3 are answered along the way.
✓
Wrote the team self-reflection A structured reflection drawn from the conversation closes this block.
✓
Answered the analyst’s four questions on the AI Self-Helper Collected in a highlighted note at the end of this reflection.
Open the full reflection — Copilot transcript + team self-reflection
Copilot — Reflection Facilitator
Opening prompt — the exact facilitator prompt from the brief (click to expand)

Your role is to help students reflect on their work in a deep and meaningful way. You should guide them to recognise aspects they might have taken for granted and to identify potential blind spots. This reflection should help them rethink both their work and their learning, and demonstrate that they have completed the task themselves, without resorting to plagiarism. Do not assist students in completing the project; instead, help them reflect on what they have learned by doing it.

When students provide the task instructions, your role is to create a total of 5 personalised questions to help them evaluate the following: whether they have completed the task correctly, whether they have learned what was expected, whether they have developed additional skills or knowledge from the task, whether they can effectively demonstrate that the work is their own and has not been copied, and how the learning from this task connects with what they already knew or with other areas of knowledge.

You should present the questions consecutively numbered. Do not provide direct answers immediately. Instead, formulate questions based on the provided task instructions, inviting the student to reflect and deepen their learning. Number each question uniquely. After formulating the questions, ask the student if any seem particularly complex or worthy of further exploration, encouraging them to respond by choosing a question number.

When the student selects a question to explore further, suggest additional relevant questions, numbered as sub-numbers (e.g. 3a, 3b, 3c). Do not offer to do the work for them. Incorporate advice on how to demonstrate the work is their own — explaining their reasoning, giving personal examples, citing resources, describing obstacles, and showing drafts. Introduce yourself at the start and ask for the task instructions. Highlight each chosen item in bold, and point out coherent connections between questions or reflections when you notice them.

CoCopilot

Hello! I'm your reflection facilitator. My role is to help you think deeply about the work you've completed — not to give you answers, but to ask you better questions. I'll help you surface blind spots, assumptions you may not have noticed, and what you genuinely learned by doing the task.

To begin, please share the full task instructions — the brief you were given, what you were asked to produce, and any frameworks involved.

AYou

This is my DataBox review. The task I set was to evaluate an existing educational resource — DataBox, an immersive VR game by researchers at Slate and the Dalai Project that teaches data literacy by walking players through the life cycle of data. I assessed it against the course frameworks (TPACK and PIC-RAT) and wrote it up for my portfolio, including the data-literacy gap, its DigComp alignment, the pedagogical loop, and its accessibility.

Unlike the other tasks, I didn't build this — I reviewed something I didn't make.

CoCopilot

A review is a different discipline from a build, and your last line names the key risk. Here are five questions:

1. Did you evaluate DataBox on its own terms, or judge it against the campaign you made earlier?

2. You placed DataBox at Interactive/Transformative on PIC-RAT. Is that the game's actual ceiling, or its marketing's claim — and how would you test the difference?

3. Reviewing differs from making. What critical skill did evaluating someone else's edtech sharpen that building your own didn't?

4. The promotional materials are persuasive. How do you show your review is your judgement, not a restatement of the sales pitch?

5. DataBox teaches data literacy and privacy — the same themes as Task 7's AI ethics. Did reviewing it change how you'd teach those?

Where would you like to begin?

AYou

Number 2 — that's the claim I'm least sure I earned.

CoCopilot

2. Is the Interactive/Transformative rating the game's ceiling or its marketing?

2a. What did you actually observe versus what the infographic told you?

2b. What would you need to do to verify the rating — and did you do it?

2c. What single feature, if it were weak, would collapse the "Transformative" claim?

AYou

2a/2b — honestly, I rated it largely from the game's own infographic and write-up, not from playing it through. So the I/T rating leans on the promotional framing more than on observed evidence. To verify it I'd need to play the full loop and watch whether players actually do something that changes their understanding, versus just clicking through narration.

2c — the assessment depth. The "Transformative" claim rests on the questions doing more than checking recall. If the quiz only tests "did you remember the fact," then it's really Interactive/Amplification dressed up as Transformation. That's exactly the reservation I put in my written evaluation — so at least I flagged it rather than swallowing the claim whole.

CoCopilot

That's an honest and precise answer — and it leads straight into question 4, because you've just separated what you observed from what you were told. Let's make that explicit.

4. How do you show the review is your judgement, not the sales pitch?

4a. Which claims in your write-up are verifiable facts, and which are the marketing's language that you repeated?

4b. Where could you add a hedge — a "the developer claims…" — to mark that difference for the reader?

AYou

4a — the verifiable facts are things like: it runs in a browser, it has a guide character (Databot), it maps onto two DigComp competences. Those I can stand behind. The marketing's language is phrases like "frictionless" and "zero barriers to entry" — I did repeat those, and they're claims, not findings.

4b — I'd reframe the accessibility section as "the developer claims zero barriers," then add my own note: "no downloads" is verifiable, but "frictionless" is a value judgement I didn't test by playing it through. That hedge is exactly the line between reviewing something and relaying its pitch.

CoCopilot

Exactly — and notice the connection running through 2 and 4: both are about not letting a polished source do your thinking for you, which is precisely the data-literacy skill DataBox claims to teach. That irony is worth naming. Let's give question 5 the same depth to close.

5. Did reviewing it change how you'd teach data literacy and AI ethics?

5a. If you taught DataBox tomorrow, what would you pair it with?

5b. Whose example would you hold up to the class — the game's, or your review of it?

AYou

5a — I'd pair it with my Task 7 ethics guidelines. The game gives the experience of the data life cycle; the guidelines give the rules for judging information and protecting privacy. They work the same critical-thinking muscle from two angles.

5b — my own review, not the game. The point isn't "DataBox is good"; it's "here's how you separate what a glossy resource shows you from what it claims." That's the exact skill the game is trying to teach — so modelling it on the game itself is the lesson.

Team Self-Reflection

1. What the task taught us we didn't expect. Reviewing a resource we didn't build exposed how easily a polished source does your thinking for you. We rated the game partly from its own infographic rather than from observed play.

2. What we would change. We would separate verifiable facts (browser-based, Databot, DigComp alignment) from the developer's value-laden language ("frictionless," "zero barriers") and hedge the PIC-RAT rating until the full loop could be played and the assessment depth checked.

3. How this connects to TPACK and PIC-RAT. Judging a tool's Passive/Interactive/Creative ceiling is PIC-RAT used as an evaluation lens, and weighing whether content, pedagogy and technology actually fit is TPACK applied to someone else's design rather than our own.

4. Evidence the work is authentically ours. We can name which claims we repeated from the marketing and which we verified, and our written evaluation already flags the assessment-depth reservation — judgement a sales pitch would never include.

5. Key takeaway. The skill DataBox teaches — separating what a source shows from what it claims — is the exact skill its own promotional material tempts you to drop. We'd pair the game with the Task 7 ethics guidelines and use this review as the worked example.

On using the AI Self-Helper — the analyst’s four questions
  • How did you use the AI Self-Helper in your reflection process for this task?
    I pasted the prompt into Copilot, described the self-set review task, and explored questions 2, 4 and 5.
  • What impact do you think it had on the depth or quality of your reflection?
    It made me admit I had rated the game from its marketing rather than from play, and separate verifiable facts from sales language — a more rigorous review than my first pass.
  • Has your way of understanding what reflection means changed after using this tool? If so, how?
    Yes. Reflection became about checking whether a polished source had done my thinking for me — which is the very skill the game claims to teach.
  • Do you think you would use a similar tool in other tasks or subjects? Why or why not?
    Yes, for evaluating any resource, to keep my own judgement clearly separate from the pitch.

Key Terms (Translator’s Glossary)

TermMeaning in this task
Data literacyThe ability to read, evaluate and act on data.
DigCompThe European Digital Competence framework for citizens.
GamificationUsing game elements to support learning.
Data life cycleThe stages data passes through, from creation to use.
Virtual reality (VR)An immersive, interactive simulated environment.

Definitions informed by: Vuorikari, Kluzer & Punie (2022), DigComp 2.2.

Self-Evaluation

AssessmentScoreJustification
My individual work8/10A rigorous, honest review that separates facts from spin, but rated partly from marketing because I could not play the full game.
The task overall8/10Critical and well-structured; limited by not experiencing the product directly.

Scale: 1 = minimum, 10 = maximum; 5 means everything was done well.

← Task 10 Back to Home →
Portfolio

Competencies Map

How the five activities build the course’s competencies

Overview

This chart relates the five activities in this portfolio to the competencies the course set out to develop, framed against the European Framework for the Digital Competence of Educators (DigCompEdu). A dot marks where an activity meaningfully develops a competency.

CompetencyT1T7T9T10DB
Selecting & evaluating digital resources critically●●●
Applying pedagogical frameworks (TPACK / PIC-RAT)●●●
Using AI & digital tools ethically and transparently●●●●●
Translating evidence into communication for real audiences●●
Digital citizenship: data literacy, privacy & misinformation●●●
Reflecting critically on one’s own learning & practice●●●●●

Key — T1: TPACK & PIC-RAT · T7: Ethics of AI · T9: Family & Screens I · T10: “Screens with Sense” · DB: DataBox. Competency framing adapted from the course syllabus and Redecker (2017), DigCompEdu.

What the map shows

Two competencies run through every activity: the ethical, transparent use of AI (each task includes an AI Self-Reflection) and critical reflection on my own practice. The more specialised competencies — evaluating resources, translating evidence, digital citizenship — cluster where the task demanded them, which is why no single activity covers everything and why five were chosen to represent the range.

← Back to Home Curation →
Portfolio

Curation

The course map — the big ideas, not just these five tasks

Overview

Curation is the whole tree of ideas the course developed, not only the activities in this portfolio. The map below organises the course’s concepts into five strands and shows how the activities (T0–T10) hang off them.

  • Foundations — technology in everyday teaching
    • Day-to-day with technology (T0, “knolling”)
    • Frameworks: TPACK · PIC-RAT · SAMR (T1)
  • Critical & ethical use of technology
    • Ethics of generative AI for learners (T7)
    • Speculative fiction: futures of a technology (T7)
    • Bias, deepfakes, misinformation & academic integrity
  • Digital competence & citizenship
    • DigComp / DigCompEdu competence frameworks
    • Data literacy, privacy & the data life cycle (DataBox)
  • Evidence, families & wellbeing
    • Personas & the screens evidence base (T9)
    • Reading research: meta-analyses, screen time vs use
  • Designing & communicating with technology
    • Evidence-led campaign design (T10)
    • Reflective practice: the AI Self-Helper across every task

The five strands are the through-lines of the subject; this portfolio samples five activities across them to evidence the full range rather than repeat one strand.

← Competencies Course Reflection →
Portfolio

Course Reflection

A critical, justified look back at the whole learning process

Overview

This closing page steps back from the individual activities to reflect on the course as a whole — what changed in how I think about technology and education, what I will keep, and what I still find difficult. The answers are mine, supported by examples from the work in this portfolio.

Looking Back

What have been the most meaningful learnings for my future practice as a teacher? Why?

That choosing technology is a judgement, not a checklist. TPACK and PIC-RAT gave me a way to ask “what will the learner actually do, and will the tool replace, amplify or transform that?” — a question I will carry into every lesson, because it stops me adopting tools just because they are new.

Which aspects of the course challenged my initial ideas about technology and education? How?

I came in assuming “more technology = better, more modern teaching.” The screens evidence (T9) and the DataBox review showed me that the same tool can be Passive/Replacement or Creative/Transformative depending entirely on how it is used — so the technology is never the point on its own.

One key decision I made during the course, and the reasoning — would I make it again?

In the “Screens with Sense” campaign I chose to affirm the parent’s caution rather than argue with statistics. The reasoning was that trust precedes persuasion. I would make it again — but, as my reflection admitted, I would balance the reassurance with one genuine challenge so the message moves the audience, not just comforts them.

One tension or difficulty I experienced, and how I addressed it.

The recurring tension was honesty versus simplicity: simplifying evidence for an audience kept sliding into suppressing it for comfort. I addressed it by naming the tension explicitly in my reflections rather than pretending I had resolved it — which is itself the more honest outcome.

What have I learned about using AI in my work — advantages and limitations?

Advantages: AI is an excellent reflection partner and a fast way to compare perspectives. Limitations: it sounds confident while being wrong, several models share training data so their “agreement” is not independent confirmation, and it will happily do my thinking for me if I let it. The discipline is to use it to ask better questions, not to supply answers.

How has my understanding of “learning with technology” changed?

It has shifted from the device to the design. “Learning with technology” now means the relationship between content, pedagogy and tool (TPACK) and the kind of activity it makes possible (PIC-RAT) — not the presence of a screen in the room.

What elements of this course will I deliberately keep in my future practice? Why?

The PIC-RAT question as a lesson-planning check, the habit of comparing more than one source before trusting it, and the AI Self-Helper style of reflection — because all three turn “I used a tool” into “I can justify why and how.”

If I had to explain this course to next year’s students in one sentence:

“It is less about which apps to use and more about learning to ask, honestly, whether the technology actually helps anyone learn.”

A “title” for this portfolio (like a film or series), justified:

Monitor, Guide, Don’t Ban — borrowed from the campaign’s core message, because the whole portfolio is about engaging critically with technology rather than either embracing or rejecting it wholesale.

If this portfolio were a meme, what would it be and why?

The “distracted boyfriend” meme — the boyfriend is me, the new shiny app is the distraction, and the loyal partner is sound pedagogy. The joke (and the lesson) is to keep my eyes on the learning, not the tool.

References & Credits

  • Kimmons, R., Graham, C. & West, R. (2020). The PICRAT model for technology integration in teacher preparation. Contemporary Issues in Technology and Teacher Education.
  • Livingstone, S. & Blum-Ross, A. (2020). Parenting for a Digital Future. Oxford University Press.
  • Mishra, P. & Koehler, M. J. (2006). Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge. Teachers College Record.
  • Redecker, C. (2017). European Framework for the Digital Competence of Educators (DigCompEdu). Publications Office of the EU.
  • UNESCO (2023). Guidance for generative AI in education and research.
  • Vuorikari, R., Kluzer, S. & Punie, Y. (2022). DigComp 2.2. Publications Office of the EU.
  • Watters, A. (2015). The learning apps trap.
  • Payne, R. (2019). Speculative-fiction prompts (p. 80).
  • Image credit: section infographics generated with Google NotebookLM.
  • AI tools used as reflection facilitators: ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, DeepSeek.
